sections.title: General Guidance
sections.content: Proper wound care helps prevent infection, reduce bleeding, and promote healing.

sections.title: Seek Medical Help Immediately If
items: Bleeding won’t stop after 10 minutes of firm pressure
Wound is deep, large, or edges are gaping
Bone, muscle, or fat is visible

steps.title: Stay Calm & Assess the Wound
steps.subtitle: Check how deep and large the wound is. Look for heavy bleeding, dirt, or embedded objects. If bleeding is severe or won’t stop after 10 minutes of pressure, seek emergency care immediately.

steps.title: Stop the Bleeding
steps.subtitle: Apply firm, direct pressure with sterile gauze or clean cloth. Elevate the injured area if possible.

steps.title: Clean the Wound
steps.subtitle: Rinse under clean running water, remove visible dirt with clean tweezers (sterilized if possible). Avoid pouring soap directly into deep wounds, use mild antiseptic solution. Avoid harsh chemicals like hydrogen peroxide for deep wounds (can damage tissue).

steps.title: Apply Dressing
steps.subtitle: Apply sterile dressing or adhesive bandage, secure with tape. Change dressing daily or when wet/dirty.

steps.title: Tetanus Protection
steps.subtitle: Ensure tetanus vaccination is up to date. Booster recommended every 10 years (or sooner for high-risk wounds).

steps.title: Monitor for Signs of Infection
steps.subtitle: Increased redness or swelling, warmth around wound. Pus or unusual discharge, fever, or red streaks spreading from wound.
